Decades of psychometric refinement have not resolved what cognitive tasks measure because the problem was never primarily psychometric – it is theoretical.

The framework developed here addresses this by making theoretical commitments explicit and testable. Using generative computational models, we can assess the validity of behavioral indicators independently of task correlations and generate falsifiable predictions about indicator-process relationships. Applied to the measurement of attention control, this framework yielded a key positive finding: RT difference scores are selectively valid indicators of AC parameters under both the DMC and SSP, providing a measurement basis that is robust across different processing assumptions. At the same time, the simulations revealed why many commonly used indicators fail to measure what they are intended to measure: Process impurity means that most behavioral indicators conflate multiple cognitive processes, leading to reliability-validity dissociations and attenuated correlation transfer for process-impure indicators. These patterns illustrate why validity assessment requires explicit process theory, not just psychometric refinement.

These contributions come with important constraints. Our simulations focused on conflict tasks; although the structural phenomena we documented arise from general features of multi-process measurement and should emerge wherever multiple parameters jointly determine behavioral indicators, demonstrating this for other domains – working memory, decision-making, learning – remains an important next step. The framework requires computational modeling expertise that is not yet widespread in individual-differences research, and well-tested generative models exist for only a subset of cognitive domains. Validity assessment through simulation establishes measurement potential under idealized conditions; whether these patterns hold in empirical data depends on model adequacy, which must be evaluated for the class of tasks and paradigms under study. Moreover, the models we used assume stationary processes and independent trials – real cognitive performance involves learning, fatigue, and strategic shifts that may alter process-indicator relationships. These limitations define the framework's current boundaries, not its ultimate reach: As generative models are developed for new domains and tested against empirical data, the approach becomes progressively more applicable.

@CronbachMeehl1955 recognized seven decades ago that establishing construct validity rests on theory testing. Computational cognitive models now make it possible to translate this theoretical insight into practice, providing the explicit process theories that their framework required but their era could not supply. Advancing validity in measuring cognition requires not better correlations, but better theories of how cognition generates behavior.

{{< pagebreak >}}

::: {#refs}
:::

{{< pagebreak >}}

# Appendix: Supplementary Methods and Results {.appendix}

## Figure A3: Model Validation – Empirical Pattern Alignment for DMC and SSP

![**Panel A:** Distribution of RT congruency effects (incongruent minus congruent mean RT, in ms) across simulated participants under the DMC (left) and SSP (right), based on the recovery simulations (*N* = 100, 100 trials per condition, 250 replications). The grey shaded band indicates the typical empirical range for flanker and Simon tasks (20–100 ms). Both models produce predominantly positive congruency effects within this range, confirming that the simulation parameter space generates behavioral data consistent with empirical conflict-task observations. **Panel B:** Distributions of mean RT for congruent (blue) and incongruent (red) conditions. Both models produce the expected pattern of slower responses in incongruent than congruent trials, with realistic RT ranges. **Panel C:** Distributions of mean accuracy for congruent (blue) and incongruent (red) conditions. Both models produce higher accuracy in congruent than incongruent trials, consistent with empirical conflict-task data [@UlrichEtAl2015; @WhiteEtAl2011; @WhiteEtAl2018].](../figures/manuscript/DMC_SSP_ModelValidation.png){#fig-modelvalidation}
